title: A Day at an Applied AI Startup
setting: A Series A or Series B applied AI startup (20–80 people), building an AI-powered product for a specific vertical such as healthcare, legal, or fintech
companySize: ML/AI team of 4–10 within a company of 20–80
description: You are one of the first ML engineers or applied researchers at a startup that is using AI to solve a real business problem. Your job is not to publish papers — it is to ship models that work reliably in production, handle edge cases gracefully, and improve measurably with each iteration. You wear multiple hats: researcher, engineer, data wrangler, and sometimes the person explaining to the CEO why the model is not ready yet. The feedback loop is tight — customers use your model today and complain about it tomorrow.
salary: $140,000 – $250,000 USD (plus equity, typically 0.1%–0.5% at Series A)

schedule.activity: Check production model monitoring dashboards — look for latency spikes, error rates, or accuracy drift
schedule.note: A customer in the healthcare vertical reported hallucinated outputs overnight — need to investigate

schedule.activity: Triage the customer-reported issue — pull the failing inputs, reproduce the error, and identify the root cause
schedule.note: The issue is a distribution shift — the customer started sending a new document format the model was not trained on

schedule.activity: Standup with the product and engineering team — prioritise the bug fix against planned feature work
schedule.note: The CEO wants the fix shipped today because the customer is on a trial that ends this week

schedule.activity: Build a quick data augmentation pipeline to handle the new document format and retrain a patched model
schedule.note: Fine-tuning on 200 new examples — need to make sure it does not degrade performance on existing formats

schedule.activity: Run evaluation benchmarks on the patched model against the existing test suite
schedule.note: Accuracy holds on existing formats, and the new format now works — but latency increased by 15%

schedule.activity: Lunch — eat at your desk while reading a blog post about quantisation techniques that might fix the latency regression
schedule.note: The GPTQ approach looks promising but would require changes to the serving infrastructure

schedule.activity: Optimise the model for inference — try ONNX export and batch size tuning to recover the latency
schedule.note: ONNX export brings latency back to acceptable levels without accuracy loss

schedule.activity: Deploy the patched model to staging and run integration tests
schedule.note: The CI pipeline catches a serialisation issue — fix it and redeploy

schedule.activity: Ship to production and monitor the first hour of traffic from the affected customer
schedule.note: The fix works — customer confirms the issue is resolved

schedule.activity: Work on a longer-term project — experimenting with retrieval-augmented generation to reduce hallucinations across all customers
schedule.note: Early results look promising but retrieval latency adds 300ms per request

schedule.activity: Review GPU costs for the month and identify experiments or serving instances that can be downsized
schedule.note: The dev cluster is running 8 A100s that nobody is using — shut them down to save $15k/month

schedule.activity: Write up the day's work in the team knowledge base and update the model card for the patched release
schedule.note: Good documentation now saves painful debugging later

responsibilities: Build, train, and deploy ML models that solve real customer problems
Monitor production model performance and respond to accuracy or reliability issues
Manage the full ML lifecycle: data collection, training, evaluation, deployment, and monitoring
Optimise model inference for cost and latency within the startup's compute budget
Collaborate with product managers to translate business requirements into ML problem formulations
Build and maintain evaluation benchmarks and automated testing for model quality
challenges: Shipping fast enough to keep customers and investors happy while maintaining model quality
GPU costs can easily exceed the startup's entire infrastructure budget if not managed carefully
Working with messy, incomplete, or biased real-world data from customers
Explaining model limitations to non-technical stakeholders who expect perfection
Balancing short-term bug fixes with long-term research that would improve the product fundamentally
Recruiting and retaining ML talent when competing with labs that pay 2x your budget
rewards: Seeing your model used by real customers solving real problems every day
The autonomy to make high-impact technical decisions without layers of approval
Equity upside if the company succeeds — early ML hires at successful AI startups have done very well
Learning across the entire stack — from data pipelines to model serving to product design
Moving fast enough that your work from Monday is in production by Friday
Building something from scratch rather than maintaining an existing system
careerPath: ML Engineer or Applied Scientist → first ML hire at an early-stage startup
Senior ML Engineer → owning a product's entire ML stack
ML Lead / Head of AI → managing a small team and setting technical direction
VP Engineering or CTO (technical track) → if the company scales
Founder → starting your own AI company based on domain expertise gained
